explanation_md: `scale` receives a copy of `v`, so changes inside the function do not affect the caller's `v`. The function prints the scaled copy (`6 10`), then `main` prints the original (`3 5`) unchanged.
`swap_pair` returns a new `Pair` with `a` and `b` exchanged. The original is passed by value, so it is unmodified. `swapped` holds the returned struct with the fields reversed.

prompt_md: When should you prefer passing a struct by `const` reference rather than by value?

options.a: When the function needs to modify the struct and have the caller see the changes.
options.b: When the function only reads the struct and you want to avoid copying every field.
options.c: When the struct has no default member initializers.
options.d: When you need the function to have its own independent copy to work with.

explanation_md: `const` reference avoids copying all the struct's fields while still preventing the function from modifying the caller's data. Option `a` describes a non-const reference. Option `d` describes pass-by-value. Default member initializers (option `c`) are unrelated to how you pass the struct.

options.a: `Counter` cannot be passed to a function.
options.b: `reset` takes `c` by value, so the change does not affect the caller's struct.
options.c: `c.count = 0` inside `reset` causes a compile error because `count` has a default initializer.
options.d: Designated initialization `{ .count = 42 }` is not valid in C++20.

explanation_md: `reset` takes `Counter` by value, meaning it operates on a local copy. Setting `c.count = 0` in the copy has no effect on the caller's object. The fix is to change the parameter to `Counter& c` (non-const reference) so the function modifies the original.
